elements.sent-2411-002: We propose a highly coarse-grained simulation model for crystalline polymer solids with crystalline lamellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-003: The mechanical properties of a crystalline polymer solid are mainly determined by the crystalline lamellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-004: This means that coarse-grained models rather than fine-scale molecular models are suitable to study mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: We model a crystalline polymer solid by using highly coarse-grained particles, of which size is comparable to the crystalline layer thickness.
elements.sent-2411-006: One coarse-grained particle consists of multiple subchains, and is much larger than monomers.
elements.sent-2411-007: Coarse-grained particles are connected by bonds to form a network structure.
elements.sent-2411-008: Particles are connected by soft but ductile bonds, to form a rubber-like network.
elements.sent-2411-009: Particles in the crystalline region are connected by hard but brittle bonds.
elements.sent-2411-010: Brittle bonds are broken when large deformations are applied.
elements.sent-2411-011: We perform uniaxial elongation simulations based on our coarse-grained model.
elements.sent-2411-012: As the applied strain increases, crystalline layers are broken into pieces and non-affine and collective motions of broken pieces are observed.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our model can successfully reproduce yield behaviors which are similar to typical crystalline polymer solids.
elements.sent-2411-014: Crystalline polymers form hierarchical structures when cooled and solidified from melt states\cite{Strobl-book,Young-Lovell-book .}
elements.sent-2411-015: At the microscopic scale, crystalline lattice structures which depend on monomer structures are formed.
elements.sent-2411-016: At the mesoscopic scale, crystals form layer-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-017: Crystalline and amorphous layers are stacked and form crystalline lamellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-018: At the larger scale, crystalline lamellae form spherulite structures.
elements.sent-2411-019: Mechanical properties of crystalline polymer solids reflect such hierarchical structures\cite{Lin-Argon-1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: A typical nominal stress-strain curve for uniaxial elongation of high density polyethelene (HDPE), which is one of widely used crystalline polymers, is shown in Figure~\ref{stress_strain_hdpe .}
elements.sent-2411-021: As the strain increases, the stress-strain curve deviates from that for a linear elastic body, and exhibits a local maximum which corresponds to the yield point.
elements.sent-2411-022: After the yield point, stress decreases and inhomogeneous neck formation and propagation are observed.
elements.sent-2411-023: The stress is almost constant during this neck propagation process.
elements.sent-2411-024: After the neck propagation is completed, the stress linearly increases with strain (the strain hardening), and finally the sample breaks.
elements.sent-2411-025: Although the details of stress-strain curves depend on various factors such as monomer structures and crystallinity, the behaviors explained above are qualitatively common for most of crystalline polymer solids\cite{Strobl-book,Young-Lovell-book .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We expect that macroscopically observed mechanical behaviors originate from microscopic and mesoscopic structural deformations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Some theoretical models have been proposed to explain macroscopic mechanical behaviors starting from microscopic or mesoscopic molecular models\cite{Lin-Argon-1994,Bowden-Young-1974,Peterlin-1987,Nitta-Takayanagi-2000,Nitta-Takayanagi-2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Different theoretical models based on different pictures give similar macroscopic mechanical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-029: To judge which model is reasonable, simultaneous measurements for structural and mechanical properties are required.
elements.sent-2411-030: To experimentally study structural deformation behaviors, scattering methods\cite{Tashiro-1993,Tashiro-Sasaki-2003,LopezBarron-Zeng-Schaefer-Eberle-Lodge-Bates-2017,Butler-Donald-1998,Jiang-Tang-Men-Enderle-Lilge-Roth-Gehrke-Rieger-2007,Millot-Seguela-Lame-Fillot-Rochas-Sotta-2017,Kishimoto-Mita-Ogawa-Takenaka-2020 as well as spectroscopic methods\cite{Tashiro-1993,Tashiro-Sasaki-2003,LopezBarron-Zeng-Schaefer-Eberle-Lodge-Bates-2017,Siesler-1980,Song-Nitta-Nemoto-2003,Kida-Oku-Hiejima-Nitta-2015,Kida-Hiejima-Nitta-2021,Kida-2022 are useful.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: By combining scattering and/or spectroscopic measurements together with mechanical measurements, we can study the relation between structural and mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-032: From scattering experiments, we have structural information such as chain conformations and orientations of crystals and lamellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-033: From spectroscopy, we have other structural informations such as orientations of chains and stresses applied to polymer chains.
elements.sent-2411-034: These informations are useful to discuss the structural deformations under large deformation.
elements.sent-2411-035: But even if we combine several experimental techniques, it is difficult to precisely determine how hierarchical structures are deformed in a real space.
elements.sent-2411-036: Simulations are also useful to study how structures are deformed and how structural deformations are related to mechanical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-037: To study structural and mechanical behaviors by simulations, molecular models have been utilized\cite{Lee-Rutledge-2011,Kim-Locker-Rutledge-2014,JabbariFarouji-Rottler-Lame-Makke-Perez-Barrat-2015,Yeh-Lenhart-Rutledge-Andzelm-2017,Higuchi-Kubo-2017,Olsson-intVeld-Andreasson-Bergvall-Jutemar-Petersson-Rutledge-Kroon-2018,Ranganathan-Kumar-Brayton-Kroger-Rutledge-2020,Hagita-Yamamoto-Saito-Abe-2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: We can directly observe real space structures in molecular simulations.
elements.sent-2411-039: Molecular dynamics (MD) models are widely used because both crystalline and amorphous structures can be naturally handled.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, nonequilibrium dynamics can be simulated by imposing deformations to simulation boxes.
elements.sent-2411-041: Various MD models, such as all-atom models\cite{Olsson-intVeld-Andreasson-Bergvall-Jutemar-Petersson-Rutledge-Kroon-2018 , slightly coarse-grained united-atom models\cite{Kim-Locker-Rutledge-2014,Yeh-Lenhart-Rutledge-Andzelm-2017,Higuchi-Kubo-2017,Ranganathan-Kumar-Brayton-Kroger-Rutledge-2020,Hagita-Yamamoto-Saito-Abe-2024 , and more coarse-grained models\cite{JabbariFarouji-Rottler-Lame-Makke-Perez-Barrat-2015 , can be used to study crystalline polymer solids.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Although it is reported that MD simulations can reproduce yield behaviors, there is serious limitation.
elements.sent-2411-043: In typical MD simulations, length and time scales are small and imposed strain rates become too high.
elements.sent-2411-044: Typically just several layers in crystalline lamellar structures are used in MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-045: This means that the structural deformations and non-affine motions at larger scales cannot be reproduced.
elements.sent-2411-046: Typical strain rates in MD simulations are $\dot{\varepsilon \approx (10^{7 \sim 10^{9 ) \text{s ^{-1}$, which is too high compared with typical values in experiments, $\dot{\varepsilon \approx 10^{-2 \text{s ^{-1}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This discrepancy becomes serious when we want to compare MD simulation data with experimental data, because both mechanical and structural behaviors of crystalline polymers strongly depend on strain rate.
elements.sent-2411-048: Simulation models which enable deformations with much lower strain rates are demanding.

elements.sent-2411-049: In this work, we propose a highly coarse-grained model for crystalline polymer solids with crystalline lamellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-050: We use a highly coarse-grained particles with a characteristic size comparable to crystal and amorphous layers.
elements.sent-2411-051: These particles are much larger than united-atoms, and thus the characteristic length and time scales of our model are much larger than those of MD models.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our model enables long-time simulations for large systems with small numerical calculation costs.
elements.sent-2411-053: To reproduce the yield behaviors, we employ the transient potential model\cite{Uneyama-2020,Uneyama-2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: In the transient potential model, potentials for coarse-grained particles are generally time-dependent and fluctuate.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the case of crystalline polymers, bond potentials in crystalline regions can be interpreted as the transient potential.
elements.sent-2411-056: Bonds can be broken spontaneously if deformed largely.
elements.sent-2411-057: We construct the potential model and dynamics model for the highly coarse-grained particles.
elements.sent-2411-058: Then, we perform uniaxial elongation simulations by using the constructed highly coarse-grained model.
elements.sent-2411-059: Simulation results show that our model can reasonably reproduce both structural and mechanical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-060: The crystalline layers are broken into pieces under large deformation.
elements.sent-2411-061: The broken pieces move non-affinely and collectively.
elements.sent-2411-062: The yield behavior associated with the breakage of crystalline layers are observed.
elements.sent-2411-063: Both structural and mechanical behaviors depend on the strain rate, and the yield stress obeys the Eyring type relation.
elements.sent-2411-064: As explained in Sec.~\ref{introduction , fine-scale MD simulations are not suitable when we are interested in the long-time scale behaviors of crystalline polymer solids with crystalline lamellar structures.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Although fine-scale MD simulations give informations on small-scale structures and dynamics, large-scale structures and dynamics are essential in many cases.
elements.sent-2411-066: To overcome this problem, we employ highly coarse-grained model where characteristic time and length scales are much larger than those of MD models.

elements.sent-2411-466: We proposed a highly coarse-grained simulation model for crystalline polymer solids.
elements.sent-2411-467: We modeled a crystalline polymer solid by using coarse-grained particles of which size is comparable to the crystalline and amorphous layers in a crystalline lamellar structure.
elements.sent-2411-468: By using such highly coarse-grained particles, our model can handle large-scale and long-time simulations with small calculation costs compared with typical MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-469: The particles are connected by two types of bonds: B-bond (brittle or breakable) and D-bond (ductile), to form a network structure.
elements.sent-2411-470: The short-range interaction potential between particles is expressed as the Hertz type contact interaction, and D-bonds are expressed as harmonic potentials.
elements.sent-2411-471: To express the interaction by B-bonds, we employed directors for coarse-grained particles.
elements.sent-2411-472: By using relative particle positrons and directors, the stretching, tilt, and bending potentials for B-bonds were formulated.
elements.sent-2411-473: As a dynamics model, simple Langevin equations and a simple breakage rule for B-bonds were employed.
elements.sent-2411-474: We performed elongation simulations with the constructed coarse-grained model, and showed that both the structural changes and mechanical behaviors can be reasonably reproduced.
elements.sent-2411-475: Simulations were performed with several different lamellar stacking directions.
elements.sent-2411-476: To estimate macroscopic properties, we calculated weighted averages of physical quantities over different lamellar stacking directions.
elements.sent-2411-477: Macroscopic two-dimensional scattering patterns and stress-strain curves can be reasonably reproduced in this method.
elements.sent-2411-478: The structural and mechanical behaviors of our model are qualitatively consistent with experiments.
elements.sent-2411-479: If the applied strain is small, B-bonds are not broken and the structures are deformed affinely.
elements.sent-2411-480: The linear elastic behavior is observed in this region.
elements.sent-2411-481: As we increase the strain and if the strain exceeds a characteristic value, B-bonds start to be broken.
elements.sent-2411-482: As a result, crystalline layers are broken into pieces.
elements.sent-2411-483: The structures are deformed non-affinely, and the yield behaviors are observed.
elements.sent-2411-484: When the strain is further increased, broken pieces of crystalline layers move collectively, in a similar way to the lamellar cluster units.
elements.sent-2411-485: In addition, the yield stress obeys the Eyring type relation.
elements.sent-2411-486: We expect that our coarse-grained model will be useful to study structural and mechanical behaviors of various crystalline polymer solids.
elements.sent-2411-487: We may be able to the buckling behavior and scattering patterns in detail, with larger-scale simulations.

elements.sent-2411-489: In this appendix, we describe details of the experiment for the stress-strain data shown in Figure~\ref{stress_strain_hdpe .}
elements.sent-2411-490: High density polyethylene (HDPE, 547999, Sigma-Aldrich) is used as received.
elements.sent-2411-491: Pellets of HDPE are pressure-molded by a mini test press (MP-2FH, Toyoseiki).
elements.sent-2411-492: The pellets are put between two copper plates coated with $0.05\text{mm}$-thick polyethylene terephthalate films.
elements.sent-2411-493: An aluminum spacer with $0.5\text{mm}$ thickness is used to control the thickness of a HDPE sheet.
elements.sent-2411-494: The pellets are pressure-molded at $180^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $10\text{MPa}$ for $5 \text{min}$.
elements.sent-2411-495: The molded HDPE sheet is quenched to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ by ice water.
elements.sent-2411-496: A dumbbell-shaped specimen (the gauge section is $5 \text{mm \times 20 \text{mm}$) is punched with a cutter (SDK-4219-19, Dumbbell).}
elements.sent-2411-497: For the thus prepared specimen, a uniaxial elongation test is performed with a universal testing machine (AGS-X, Shimadzu).
elements.sent-2411-498: A $500 \text{N}$ load cell is used, and a contact-type extensometer (DSES-1000, Shimadzu) with a $20 \text{mm}$ gauge bar is used to measure the actual elongation of the gauge section.
elements.sent-2411-499: The elongation speed is $20 \text{mm /\text{min}$, and the corresponding nominal strain rate is $1.6 \times 10^{-2 \text{s ^{-1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-500: The test is conducted at the room temperature.
elements.sent-2411-501: From the measured stress-strain curve, several mechanical parameters are estimated.
elements.sent-2411-502: The Young's modulus is estimated as $6.07 \times 10^{2 \text{MPa}$ (from data points for $\varepsilon < 0.01$), the yield strain and stress are estimated as $\varepsilon_{y = 0.14$ and $\sigma_{y = 15.4\text{MPa}$ (from the local maximum), and the strain and stress at break are estimated as $\varepsilon_{b = 10.7$ and $\sigma_{b = 28.0\text{MPa}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-605: In this appendix, we show the method to calculate the average over different lamellar stacking directions.
elements.sent-2411-606: A macroscopic specimen will be divided into small mesoscopic regions so that the lamellar stacking direction in one mesoscopic region is almost constant.
elements.sent-2411-607: As a rough approximation, we assume that all the mesoscopic regions are deformed affinely when the specimen is deformed.
elements.sent-2411-608: Then macroscopic physical quantities can be interpreted as the averages over different lamellar stacking directions.
elements.sent-2411-609: We express the unit normal vector to the lamellar stacking direction of a domain as $\bm{n}$ ($|\bm{n | = 1$).}
elements.sent-2411-610: If the lamellar stacking direction is random, $\bm{n}$ can be interpreted as a random variable of which probability distribution is given as $P(\bm{n ) = 1 / 4 \pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-611: Because we are considering the uniaxial elongation, it is convenient to use the spherical coordinates: \begin{equation}n_{x} = \cos \varphi \sin \vartheta, \qquad n_{y} = \sin \varphi \sin \vartheta, \qquad n_{z} = \cos \vartheta,\end{equation} where $0 < \varphi \le 2 \pi$ and $0 \le \vartheta \le \phi$.
elements.sent-2411-612: If a physical quantity $A(\vartheta)$ depends only on $\vartheta$, and has the symmetry $A(\pi - \vartheta) = A(\vartheta)$, the average over $\bm{u}$ is calculated as \begin{equation}\label{physical_quantity_average_over_theta} \bar{A} = \int_{0}^{\pi} d\vartheta \int_{0}^{2 \pi} d\varphi \, \sin \vartheta \frac{1}{4 \pi} A(\vartheta) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} d\vartheta \, \sin \vartheta A(\vartheta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-613: From the symmetry, we have limited the range for $\vartheta$ to $0 \le \vartheta \le \pi / 2$.
elements.sent-2411-614: In simulations, we can calculate values only for limited number of $\vartheta$.
elements.sent-2411-615: In this work, we use $\vartheta = \theta_{i = i \pi / 12$ ($i = 0,1,2,\dots, 6$).}
elements.sent-2411-616: We express the physical quantity calculated for the $i$-th direction as $A_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-617: Then we approximate eq~\eqref{physical_quantity_average_over_theta as \begin{equation}\label{physical_quantity_average_over_theta_approx} \bar{A} \approx \sum_{i = 0}^{6} \int_{(\theta_{i} + \theta_{i - 1}) / 2}^{(\theta_{i + 1} + \theta_{i}) / 2} d\vartheta \, \sin \theta_{i} A_{i},\end{equation} with $\theta_{-1 = \theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{7 = \theta_{6}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-618: Due to the factor $\sin \theta_{i}$ in the integrand, the weight depends on the direction rather strongly.
elements.sent-2411-619: We can define the weight factor for the $i$-th direction as \begin{equation}\label{weight_factor_for_average} W_{i} = \int_{(\theta_{i} + \theta_{i - 1}) / 2}^{(\theta_{i + 1} + \theta_{i}) / 2} d\vartheta \, \sin \theta_{i} = \cos\left(\frac{\theta_{i} + \theta_{i - 1}}{2}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{\theta_{i + 1} - \theta_{i}}{2}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-620: Then eq~\eqref{physical_quantity_average_over_theta_approx can be simply rewritten as \begin{equation}\label{physical_quantity_average_over_theta_approx_simplified} \bar{A} \approx \sum_{i = 0}^{6} W_{i} A_{i}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-622: As we explained, the weight factor depends strongly on the direction.
elements.sent-2411-623: The contributions of $\theta_{0}$ and $\theta_{1}$ to the average are small.
